old_value: {"id":"Demarch-09n","title":"[interfere] Enable Track B5 shadow mode in Clavain routing.yaml","description":"Set local_models.mode: shadow in os/Clavain/config/routing.yaml so Clavain logs which tasks would route to local inference without actually routing them. This is the safe first step before enforce mode.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T14:39:50Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T14:39:50Z"}

Server runnable via 'uv run python -m server [--dry-run]'. InferenceEngine verified with mlx-community/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct-4bit. 18/18 tests pass. Changes local only — no git repo yet (Demarch-26p).

new_value: Metal worker wired to InferenceEngine. Full pipeline: HTTP → Metal subprocess → mlx-lm stream_generate → SSE. Verified e2e with Qwen2.5-0.5B. Commit 188e350.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-llc","title":"[interfere] Update model tier routing to MoE-first models","description":"Replace the current complexity-to-model mapping in routing.yaml and AGENTS.md with newer MoE models that are significantly faster and better quality. Current: qwen3-8b/qwen3-30b/qwen2.5-72b. Proposed: Qwen3.5-9B-OptiQ-4bit (C1 draft), Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 4-bit (C2, MoE 3B active), Nemotron-Cascade-2-30B-A3B 8-bit or gpt-oss-120b MXFP4 (C3). MoE models give big-model quality at small-model speed because only a fraction of params are active per token. Also update the memory budget in AGENTS.md to reflect actual model sizes.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:15Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:15Z"}

new_value: Updated routing.yaml tier_mappings and complexity_routing to MoE-first models (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Nemotron-Cascade-2-30B-A3B, gpt-oss-120b). Updated AGENTS.md memory budget. Clavain commit 145a83b, interfere commit 0e9e57b.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-5pa","title":"[interfere] Investigate flash-moe integration for 397B MoE inference","description":"Research integrating flash-moe (github.com/danveloper/flash-moe) into the interfere pipeline. flash-moe is a pure C/Metal engine that runs Qwen3.5-397B-A17B by streaming expert weights from SSD. On a 48GB M3 Max it achieves 4.36 tok/s at 4-bit. On our 128GB M5 Max, expert caching would be dramatically better (most active experts stay in RAM), potentially 8-12+ tok/s. Integration options: (1) subprocess bridge calling flash-moe binary, (2) port SSD-streaming techniques into our MLX pipeline, (3) hybrid where flash-moe handles C3+ tasks and mlx-lm handles C1-C2. This would be a game-changer for sovereignty — running a 397B model locally. Should be an interlab experiment campaign.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:19Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:19Z"}

new_value: Feasibility study complete. M5 Max SSD benchmarks: 36 GB/s seq, 27 GB/s random pread (1.5-2x faster than M3 Max test rig). Estimated 7-10 tok/s for 397B model at 4-bit. Recommend subprocess bridge integration. Doc: docs/investigations/2026-03-26-flash-moe-feasibility.md

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-6c9","title":"[interfere] Wire experiment infrastructure: config, benchmark harness, interlab integration","description":"Build the missing plumbing that all experiments need: (1) config loader that reads experiment toggles from YAML/env vars, (2) benchmark harness that runs a standard prompt corpus and measures tok/s, TTFT, quality, (3) interlab campaign integration for baseline/treatment comparison. Currently experiments exist as hook skeletons but have no way to be configured, benchmarked, or compared systematically.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:35:05Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:35:05Z"}

new_value: Experiment infrastructure shipped: YAML config loader with env overrides, 8-prompt benchmark harness with tok/s+TTFT+thermal measurement, CLI with --save. 29/29 tests. First benchmark: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B at 85.8 tok/s median. Commit aed5765.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-875","title":"[interfere] Wire experiment hooks into InferenceEngine generate loop","description":"Connect EarlyExitHook and ReservoirReadout into InferenceEngine.generate() so they actually fire during inference. Currently they exist as tested skeletons but are never called. Need: (1) pass experiment config into InferenceEngine, (2) call early_exit.check() after each token's logits, (3) call reservoir_routing.classify() on hidden states for routing metadata, (4) expose hook stats via /health endpoint. This unblocks all experiment campaigns (Demarch-1qa).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:48:12Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:48:12Z"}

new_value: Experiment hooks wired into InferenceEngine. EarlyExitHook measures per-token confidence via logprobs. Config via YAML + env vars. Metrics in /health and generation responses. 29/29 tests pass. Commit 14ba134.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-qdg","title":"[interfere] Experiment: confidence cascade with multi-model waterfall","description":"Implement the confidence cascade described in routing.yaml: start inference with the smallest model, measure first-3-token average probability. If confidence \u003e 0.8: accept. If 0.6-0.8: try next larger model. If \u003c 0.6: escalate to cloud. This is the quality gate for local-vs-cloud routing — the key mechanism that makes sovereignty practical. Requires: (1) token-level probability extraction from mlx-lm, (2) fast model switching in the Metal worker, (3) latency budget tracking (cascade can't be slower than just calling the big model).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:23:45Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:23:45Z"}

new_value: Benchmark complete: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 4-bit — 85.8 tok/s median, 114 tok/s coding, 155ms TTFT. Results saved.
Confidence cascade implemented: multi-model waterfall with accept/escalate/cloud thresholds. 36/36 tests. Commit e39ccb7.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-9hx","title":"[interfere] Experiment: prompt caching across agent sessions","description":"Clavain agents share substantial prompt prefixes (system prompts, CLAUDE.md context, skill injections). mlx-lm supports prompt caching. Implement: (1) hash-based prefix cache that persists across requests, (2) measure cache hit rate for typical Clavain workflows (brainstorm→plan→execute share ~80% prefix), (3) integrate with the priority queue to deduplicate shared prefixes. Expected: 40-60% TTFT reduction for second and subsequent agent requests in a sprint.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:24:15Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:24:15Z"}

new_value: Prompt cache manager implemented: hash-based prefix lookup, LRU eviction, disk-persisted KV states via mlx-lm save_prompt_cache. CacheStats for hit rate tracking. 43/43 tests. Commit b15e420.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-dw8","title":"[interfere] Experiment: quantized KV cache (Q8K/Q4V)","description":"Quantize the Key-Value cache to reduce memory pressure. Keys to 8-bit, Values to 4-bit (asymmetric quantization since values are more tolerant). KIVI (2024) shows 2-bit KV is feasible. ThinK (2024) shows channel pruning in KV cache gives 20% reduction composable with quantization. On 128GB M5 Max this could: (1) fit larger KV context windows, (2) leave more RAM for page cache when SSD-streaming experts, (3) enable running 397B with larger batch sizes. The AGENTS.md already references Q8K/Q4V in the memory budget.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:56Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:56Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## KV Cache Quantization Benchmark Results (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-4bit, M5 Max 128GB)

### 100-token runs (warm cache)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 85.8 | 80.3 | 43.1/110.4 | 0.155s |
| kv_bits=8 | 86.0 | 81.7 | 44.1/111.4 | 0.155s |
| kv_bits=4 | 81.6 | 75.8 | 35.8/103.9 | 0.171s |
| kv_bits=2 | 79.7 | 78.3 | 42.1/106.4 | 0.160s |

### 500-token runs (thermal throttled)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 53.8 | 67.1 | 27.5/122.5 | 0.218s |
| kv_bits=8 | 57.9 | 65.4 | 26.1/112.7 | 0.211s |
| kv_bits=4 | 53.3 | 59.0 | 25.1/110.1 | 0.247s |
| kv_bits=2 | 49.8 | 54.7 | 25.9/98.9 | 0.236s |

### Key findings:
1. **Quality: zero degradation at all levels** — factual, reasoning, and instruction outputs identical through kv_bits=2
2. **kv_bits=8: free lunch** — no throughput penalty, 2x KV memory reduction
3. **kv_bits=4: ~5% throughput cost** — 4x KV memory reduction, worth it for larger models
4. **kv_bits=2: ~7% throughput cost** — 8x KV memory reduction, quality still holds at 500 tokens
5. Thermal throttling dominates at 500-token runs (heavy thermal state), making speed differences noisy
6. The real win is memory headroom for 122B/397B models, not speed on the 35B

### Recommendation: default to kv_bits=8 for all models, use kv_bits=4 for 122B+"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-dw8","title":"[interfere] Experiment: quantized KV cache (Q8K/Q4V)","description":"Quantize the Key-Value cache to reduce memory pressure. Keys to 8-bit, Values to 4-bit (asymmetric quantization since values are more tolerant). KIVI (2024) shows 2-bit KV is feasible. ThinK (2024) shows channel pruning in KV cache gives 20% reduction composable with quantization. On 128GB M5 Max this could: (1) fit larger KV context windows, (2) leave more RAM for page cache when SSD-streaming experts, (3) enable running 397B with larger batch sizes. The AGENTS.md already references Q8K/Q4V in the memory budget.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:56Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T17:44:12Z"}

new_value: Benchmarked kv_bits={2,4,8,none} on 35B at 100 and 500 tokens. Quality identical at all levels. kv8 is free lunch, kv4 ~5% cost, kv2 ~7% cost. Plumbed kv_bits through full stack: InferenceEngine → MetalWorker → benchmark_cli.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-8t0","title":"[interfere] Benchmark Qwen3.5-122B-A10B 4-bit via mlx-lm","description":"Run the downloaded Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-4bit through interfere's server. Measure: tok/s, time-to-first-token, quality on standard prompts. 122B total, 10B active, ~65GB, fits in RAM on 128GB M5 Max.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:15:11Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:15:11Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## 122B Benchmark Results (Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-4bit, M5 Max 128GB)

### 100-token runs
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 49.9 | 45.7 | 26.0/59.0 | 0.225s |
| kv_bits=8 | 46.0 | 44.0 | 25.8/56.7 | 0.240s |
| kv_bits=4 | 33.0 | 30.7 | 18.5/37.7 | 0.341s |

### 500-token run (baseline only)
| Median tok/s | Mean | TTFT | Thermal |
|-------------|------|------|---------|
| 24.6 | 26.4 | 0.492s | heavy |

### Comparison: 35B vs 122B
| Metric | 35B (3B active) | 122B (10B active) |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Median tok/s (100t) | 85.8 | 49.9 |
| TTFT | 0.155s | 0.225s |
| RAM footprint | ~19GB | ~65GB |
| Quality | Good | Better (cleaner reasoning) |

### Key findings:
1. **50 tok/s is usable** for real-time generation — comparable to cloud latency
2. kv_bits=8 has slight penalty on 122B (~8%) vs free lunch on 35B — larger model = more KV computation
3. kv_bits=4 has significant penalty on 122B (~34%) — not recommended at short context
4. 500-token runs hit thermal throttling hard (nominal→heavy, 50→25 tok/s)
5. 122B quality visibly better than 35B on reasoning tasks"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-8t0","title":"[interfere] Benchmark Qwen3.5-122B-A10B 4-bit via mlx-lm","description":"Run the downloaded Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-4bit through interfere's server. Measure: tok/s, time-to-first-token, quality on standard prompts. 122B total, 10B active, ~65GB, fits in RAM on 128GB M5 Max.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:15:11Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T18:20:46Z"}

new_value: Benchmarked 122B at 100 and 500 tokens. 49.9 tok/s median (100t), 24.6 tok/s (500t, thermal-limited). Quality better than 35B. kv8 has ~8% penalty (not free like on 35B). Fits in RAM at 65GB on 128GB M5 Max.

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-bu3","title":"[interfere] Experiment: speculative decoding with small draft model","description":"Use Qwen3.5-9B as a draft model to accelerate generation from larger models (122B, 397B). Speculative decoding generates candidate tokens with the fast model, then verifies in parallel with the target model. Expected: 2-2.5x speedup (per Leviathan et al. 2023). mlx-lm had a speculative_decoding example. Measure: tok/s improvement, rejection rate, quality parity.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:36Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:36Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Speculative Decoding Results: 35B draft → 122B target

### Results (M5 Max 128GB)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | TTFT | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|------|------|-------------|
| 122B alone | 49.9 | 45.7 | 0.225s | — |
| +35B draft (3 tok/step) | 14.9 | 14.9 | 0.688s | **3.3x slower** |
| +35B draft (5 tok/step) | 11.4 | 11.4 | 0.909s | **4.4x slower** |

### Why it failed:
1. **Draft model too large**: 35B (19GB) is not small enough — ideal draft is 10-20x smaller
2. **Memory bandwidth saturated**: 84GB total model weight leaves minimal headroom
3. **MoE active ratio too close**: 3B active (draft) vs 10B active (target) — only 3.3x gap
4. **Thermal throttling**: heavy state throughout, both models competing for bandwidth

### What would work:
- Need a ~1-3B draft model with matching Qwen3.5 tokenizer
- Qwen3.5-9B-4bit (~5GB) would be ideal if available on mlx-community
- Alternative: self-speculative decoding (LayerSkip, Demarch-qbv) avoids separate draft entirely"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-bu3","title":"[interfere] Experiment: speculative decoding with small draft model","description":"Use Qwen3.5-9B as a draft model to accelerate generation from larger models (122B, 397B). Speculative decoding generates candidate tokens with the fast model, then verifies in parallel with the target model. Expected: 2-2.5x speedup (per Leviathan et al. 2023). mlx-lm had a speculative_decoding example. Measure: tok/s improvement, rejection rate, quality parity.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:36Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T19:14:32Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Speculative Decoding — Full Results (M5 Max 128GB)

### 0.8B draft → 35B target (100 tokens)
| Config | Median tok/s | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 35B alone | 85.8 | — |
| +0.8B draft (3 tok/step) | 76.0 | **-11%** |

### 0.8B draft → 122B target (100 tokens)
| Config | Median tok/s | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 122B alone | 49.9 | — |
| +0.8B draft (2 tok/step) | 36.4 | -27% (thermal confound) |
| +0.8B draft (3 tok/step) | 46.9 | **-6%** |
| +0.8B draft (5 tok/step) | 40.8 | -18% |

### 35B draft → 122B target (100 tokens)
| +35B draft (3 tok/step) | 14.9 | **-70%** |
| +35B draft (5 tok/step) | 11.4 | -77% |

### Key findings:
1. **Speculative decoding does not help on M5 Max** — all configurations slower than baseline
2. **Root cause: memory bandwidth bottleneck** — Apple Silicon unified memory means spec dec trades compute savings (which we don't need) for extra memory traffic (which is our bottleneck)
3. **Dense→MoE mismatch hurts acceptance rate** — 0.8B dense draft generates different tokens from MoE target
4. **Larger draft = more waste** — 35B draft is catastrophically slow
5. **Optimal if using at all**: 0.8B draft with 3 tokens/step (only -6% on 122B)
6. **Self-speculative (LayerSkip) is the right path** for Apple Silicon — no extra model, no extra memory traffic"}

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-qbv","title":"[interfere] Experiment: LayerSkip / self-speculative early exit","description":"Implement early exit with self-speculative verification (LayerSkip, arXiv 2404.16710). Early layers generate draft tokens, remaining layers verify. No separate draft model needed — uses the target model's own early layers. Expected: 1.8-2.2x speedup. This extends our existing early_exit.py hook from entropy-based exit to self-speculative verification. Key advantage: shares compute/activations between draft and verify, reducing memory vs standard speculative decoding.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:37Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:37Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Confidence Profile Results (Early Exit Calibration)

### Tokens above confidence thresholds
| Model | >80% | >90% | >95% | >99% |
|-------|------|------|------|------|
| 0.8B dense | 71.4% | 64.2% | 56.8% | 44.8% |
| 35B MoE (3B active) | 81.7% | 76.1% | 71.0% | 64.4% |
| 122B MoE (10B active) | 84.1% | 78.7% | 74.0% | 67.5% |

### Key findings:
1. **Larger models are MORE confident** — higher fraction of easy tokens
2. **67.5% of 122B tokens have >99% confidence** — huge LayerSkip potential
3. **Creative writing has lowest confidence** (41-60% >99%) — layer skipping should be conservative here
4. **Coding and instruction following are most confident** (67-84% >99%) — best candidates for early exit
5. **MoE architecture concentrates confidence** — expert routing already "simplifies" many tokens

### Theoretical speedup if LayerSkip could skip 50% of layers for >99% tokens:
- 122B: 67.5% × 0.5 = 33.75% compute saved → ~1.5x speedup (50→75 tok/s)
- 35B: 64.4% × 0.5 = 32.2% compute saved → ~1.5x speedup (86→129 tok/s)"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-qbv","title":"[interfere] Experiment: LayerSkip / self-speculative early exit","description":"Implement early exit with self-speculative verification (LayerSkip, arXiv 2404.16710). Early layers generate draft tokens, remaining layers verify. No separate draft model needed — uses the target model's own early layers. Expected: 1.8-2.2x speedup. This extends our existing early_exit.py hook from entropy-based exit to self-speculative verification. Key advantage: shares compute/activations between draft and verify, reducing memory vs standard speculative decoding.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:37Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T19:29:25Z"}

{"id":"Demarch-1qa","title":"[interfere] Start interlab experiment campaigns for esoteric techniques","description":"Create interlab campaigns for each experiment: early exit (entropy-based layer skipping), reservoir routing (frozen-layer MLP), and any other planned techniques. Define baselines, success metrics, and kill conditions per AGENTS.md experiment specs.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T14:39:57Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T14:39:57Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Interlab Campaign Assessment

Current interfere experiments are measurement/characterization, not optimization — autoresearch shines at iterative code changes with measurable metrics.

### kv_group_size sweep (manual):
- {32, 64, 128} all within noise (85.8-91.9 tok/s on 35B with kv_bits=8)
- MLX only supports {32, 64, 128}. Not worth autoresearch.

### When to launch autoresearch:
1. After LayerSkip implementation — tune exit threshold, layer skip pattern
2. After TurboQuant kernel — tune PolarQuant grid parameters
3. Prompt template optimization — tune chat template for MoE expert routing"}

new_value:
{"description":"Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize TurboQuant KV cache parameters.

**Primary metric**: kv_memory_bytes (lower_is_better)
**Quality gate**: perplexity delta vs fp16 KV ≤ 0.1
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-turboquant-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/experiments/turboquant.py`, `server/kv_quantize.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - PolarQuant grid resolution: 4–16 points on unit circle
  - PolarQuant coordinate mapping: cartesian vs polar vs hybrid
  - QJL projection dimension: 32–256
  - QJL residual bits: 1–2
  - Per-layer vs uniform quantization
  - Asymmetric K/V bit allocation (e.g., 3-bit K + 2-bit V)
**Kill condition**: 10 experiments with no memory reduction at iso-quality
**Prerequisite**: TurboQuant MLX kernel must exist first (sylveste-4dy)

Baseline (from Demarch-dw8):
  - kv_bits=8: free lunch on 35B, ~8% cost on 122B
  - kv_bits=4: ~5% cost on 35B, ~34% cost on 122B
  - kv_bits=2: ~7% cost on 35B, quality still identical
  - TurboQuant targets 3-bit with ZERO quality loss"}

{"description":"Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize page cache hit rate for SSD-streamed MoE inference.

**Primary metric**: cache_hit_rate_pct (higher_is_better)
**Secondary metric**: tok_s on 397B model
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-pagecache-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/ssd_streaming.py`, `server/expert_prefetch.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - madvise hint strategy: MADV_WILLNEED vs MADV_SEQUENTIAL vs MADV_RANDOM
  - Pre-warm depth: 0 (none), 1 (next predicted expert), 2 (next 2), 3
  - Prediction method: last-token routing scores vs moving average vs markov chain
  - Memory wiring threshold: wire top-N most-used experts (N=0..16)
  - Page cache reservation: 0GB (OS default), 16GB, 32GB, 48GB
  - Expert access reordering: original order vs frequency-sorted vs clustering
**Kill condition**: 10 experiments with <1% cache hit improvement
**Prerequisite**: flash-moe baseline benchmark (sylveste-4l2) + 397B download

Baseline from flash-moe investigation:
  - M5 Max SSD: 36 GB/s sequential, 27 GB/s random pread
  - Expected ~71% cache hit rate without optimization (flash-moe M3 baseline)
  - Target: 85%+ cache hit rate with smart pre-fetching"}

{"description":"Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize concurrent request batching.

**Primary metric**: aggregate_tok_s (higher_is_better, sum across N clients)
**Quality gate**: P99 latency ≤ 2x single-request latency
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-batching-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/batch_scheduler.py`, `server/metal_worker.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - Batch accumulation timeout: 5ms–100ms
  - Max batch size: 2–8 sequences
  - Priority weight for high-pri requests: 1.0–10.0
  - Preemption threshold: never / after-prefill / after-N-tokens
  - Sequence padding strategy: left-pad vs right-pad vs no-pad
**Kill condition**: 10 experiments with no throughput gain vs sequential
**Prerequisite**: Batch scheduler implementation (sylveste-5ne)

Context: MLX has no concurrent inference (ml-explore/mlx#3078).
Batching works within the sequential constraint by sharing prefill computation.
Common case: 3-5 concurrent Clavain subagents during flux-drive reviews."}

new_value: {"description":"Google's TurboQuant (ICLR 2026, arxiv.org/abs/2504.19874) achieves 3-bit KV cache quantization with zero accuracy loss on long-context benchmarks. Two-stage approach: (1) PolarQuant converts KV to polar coordinates, maps to fixed circular grid (eliminates expensive normalization), (2) QJL adds 1-bit residual error correction via Johnson-Lindenstrauss Transform. Results: 6x KV memory reduction, 8x performance increase on H100 GPUs vs 32-bit. Tested on Gemma, Mistral, Llama-3.1-8B. This is strictly better than our planned Q8K/Q4V approach (sylveste-dw8) — 3-bit with ZERO quality loss vs 4-8 bit with potential degradation. For interfere: (1) implement PolarQuant for MLX/Metal, (2) measure KV savings on Qwen3.5-35B and 122B, (3) combine with StreamingLLM sinks for near-infinite context. Companion paper: PolarQuant (AISTATS 2026, arxiv.org/abs/2502.02617)."}

new_value: NEGATIVE RESULT: Polar coordinate KV cache quantization does NOT work. Three iterations: (1) Original wrapper called inverse_polar_transform on quantized tuples — structurally broken. (2) Fixed dequantize-then-inverse with interleaved layout — 3.1x worse error. (3) Contiguous r/theta layout — 1.19x worse at 4-bit, still garbage output at ALL bit widths (2,4,8). Root cause: inverse trig transform amplifies quantization noise by 2.7x — angle error delta_theta becomes Cartesian error r*sin(delta_theta), proportional to radius. High-radius vectors (most important for attention) suffer most. This is a fundamental mathematical limitation. Kept code for reference.

old_value:
{"id":"sylveste-bhq","title":"[interfere] TurboQuant v3: custom Lloyd-Max quantizer + attention kernel for MLX","description":"Implement TurboQuant properly with custom Lloyd-Max centroids optimized for the post-rotation Beta distribution, plus a custom Metal attention kernel that uses non-uniform dequantization. This is the full paper-faithful implementation that v1 (polar) and v2 (rotation + affine) proved cannot be done with MLX's native quantization alone.

Key components:
1. Lloyd-Max 1D quantizer: precompute optimal centroids for Beta(d/2, d/2) distribution at each bit width (2,3,4,8). Store as lookup table.
2. Custom quantize/dequantize: per-coordinate centroid lookup instead of uniform grid + scale/bias.
3. Custom attention kernel: replaces mx.quantized_matmul with centroid-aware dequantize-then-matmul (or fused Metal kernel if performance requires).
4. Random orthogonal rotation matrix (QR decomposition) applied to K before quantization, Q at attention time.
5. Optional QJL residual correction for sub-3-bit.

Prerequisites: v2 experiment proved rotation works mathematically (fp16 path identical) but MLX affine quant negates the benefit. This bead is the proper implementation.

Kill condition: if custom Metal kernel is >2x slower than native mx.quantized_matmul at same bit width, abandon.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-27T14:56:17Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-27T14:56:17Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Phase 1-2 COMPLETE (2026-03-27): Lloyd-Max solver, BHQ quantize/dequantize, BHQCacheWrapper with norm preservation, non-fused attention, benchmark CLI, 14 tests passing. Results: 2-bit BHQ 36% better than native, 3-bit 12% better, 4-bit native wins by 14%. Speed parity (no fused kernel needed). End-to-end generation on 0.5B-4bit model degrades — needs larger model validation. Phase 3 (fused Metal kernel) NOT needed per kill condition."}
old_value:
{"id":"sylveste-bhq","title":"[interfere] TurboQuant v3: custom Lloyd-Max quantizer + attention kernel for MLX","description":"Implement TurboQuant properly with custom Lloyd-Max centroids optimized for the post-rotation Beta distribution, plus a custom Metal attention kernel that uses non-uniform dequantization. This is the full paper-faithful implementation that v1 (polar) and v2 (rotation + affine) proved cannot be done with MLX's native quantization alone.

Key components:
1. Lloyd-Max 1D quantizer: precompute optimal centroids for Beta(d/2, d/2) distribution at each bit width (2,3,4,8). Store as lookup table.
2. Custom quantize/dequantize: per-coordinate centroid lookup instead of uniform grid + scale/bias.
3. Custom attention kernel: replaces mx.quantized_matmul with centroid-aware dequantize-then-matmul (or fused Metal kernel if performance requires).
4. Random orthogonal rotation matrix (QR decomposition) applied to K before quantization, Q at attention time.
5. Optional QJL residual correction for sub-3-bit.

Prerequisites: v2 experiment proved rotation works mathematically (fp16 path identical) but MLX affine quant negates the benefit. This bead is the proper implementation.

Kill condition: if custom Metal kernel is >2x slower than native mx.quantized_matmul at same bit width, abandon.","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-27T14:56:17Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-27T15:02:22Z"}

new_value: Phase 1-2 complete: Lloyd-Max solver, BHQ cache wrapper with norm preservation, non-fused attention, benchmark CLI, 14 tests. Phase 3 (fused Metal kernel) not needed — speed is within kill condition. Quality validation on larger model tracked as sylveste-d5e.

new_value: {"notes":"VALIDATED on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit (head_dim=128, GQA 8:1, 36 layers). BHQ-4 produces COHERENT text while Native-4 produces GARBAGE. BHQ-4=56 tps, Native-4=82 tps, FP16=77 tps. BHQ 30% slower but only quantized mode with usable text. VERDICT: BHQ viable for head_dim\u003e=128."}
old_value: {"id":"sylveste-d5e","title":"[interfere] BHQ validation: test on 7B+ model with head_dim=128","description":"Validate BHQ (TurboQuant v3) quality on a larger model where the Beta distribution concentrates better (head_dim=128 vs 64). The 0.5B test model has head_dim=64 and 4-bit weight quantization — double quantization is too lossy. Test with Qwen 7B or Llama 8B at 2-3 bit KV to confirm the synthetic attention NMSE advantage translates to coherent generation. If 3-bit BHQ produces quality comparable to 4-bit native on 7B+, BHQ enables 25% KV cache memory savings.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-27T15:37:50Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-27T15:37:50Z","labels":["artifact_plan:docs/plans/2026-03-27-bhq-validation-7b.md"]}

new_value: VALIDATED: BHQ-4 produces coherent text on Qwen2.5-3B (head_dim=128) while Native-4 produces garbage. BHQ is viable for KV cache quantization at head_dim>=128. Speed gap (30%) tracked as sylveste-naj.
Shipped: routing config, generate lock, prompt cache, playtest bridge, computer-use research
